Adventure 3. Guys and Dolls My first visit to Tom's Garage had started with a loud noise. The second with a mouthwatering aroma. This next one began with an unusual visual impression. Eight colorful wooden Russian dolls neatly lined up on a workbench, each one just a little smaller than the next. This is a Russian nesting doll, a Matryoshka, Tom explained. Each one fits inside the other. It's a beautiful illustration of my sixth box. Put one inside the other. In engineering it's a great space saver. You put something inside something else, or you nestle many things on top of each other, like a telescoping antenna, storing lead in a lead pencil, or folding chairs, or paper cups nesting into one another. Here's a little problem I confronted recently for an automotive manufacturer. They were at their wits end over, of all things, cup holders. They wanted a container capable of holding anything, from a small cup of coffee to a huge big gulp or water bottle. But when it was snug enough to hold the small coffee, the big water bottle wouldn't fit. When it was big enough to hold the big water bottle, the small cup of coffee would bounce around and tip over. They tried all sorts of flexible lips and grips, but nothing seemed to work. I went through a couple of boxes and quickly came to put one thing inside the other. Ah, I said, you had them put the coffee in the big water bottle or the water in the small coffee cup. I joked. That would solve the problem. Indeed, it would, sniggered Tom. In a way. But it was that kind of thinking that led me to the solution. I started out with a large hole that could fit the biggest big gulp or water bottle. Then I designed a series of smaller and smaller cup-like inserts that fastened securely into the big hole and into each other, creating smaller and smaller openings. The driver or passenger could create an opening that was just the right size for their drink, simply by adding or removing inserts. I nodded, in admiration of his clever thought process. And I said, you never would have come up with that solution if you hadn't known about the Russian dolls and systematically tried to see if that idea would apply to the situation. Exactly, said Tom, pointing his index finger in the air. You know, nesting one thing inside another has been applied to the bookstore industry with quite some success. Do you know what I'm talking about? Putting a café within a bookstore? I ventured. Yes! What a brilliant idea that was. Way back when someone had the thought to sell a cup of coffee in a bookstore or a book in a coffee shop. What an effective symbiotic relationship. People who come for the bookstore also spend money at the café, and people who come for the café also spend money at the bookstore. Of course, you've already got a café, but is there any other way you could use this box? Any other way you could nest one thing inside another to help make your bookstore generate more profits? I thought for a moment, I could do like the chains do, and add a gift shop within the bookstore, offering book-related and even non-book-related items. This would give people another reason to come in and potentially attract a younger crowd. And a nice little bonus is that there's a much better markup on that kind of stuff than on books. I could even put in some kind of supervised play area for kids. There's nothing worse than trying to decide whether or not to buy a book with a bored three-year-old tugging at your pants. Good, said Tom. Now, shift to another level. Another level? Yes. One of the beauties of these boxes is that they work at many different levels. You can use them to look at things at a macro, big picture level, or on a more detailed micro level. You can interpret these questions tangibly, such as literally putting one thing inside another or more metaphorically, such as putting one idea inside another. You've been thinking at one level how to put some fairly big elements, such as a music or gift shop, inside the entire element, the bookstore. Now, try shifting your perspective. What things can you put inside different parts of the bookstore? Putting things inside things. I closed my eyes and imagined zooming in on my bookstore. Well, we've got plenty of books. What if I put something in the books? I keep reading that the more time people spend looking at books in the store, the more they purchase. So maybe I could put something inside the books that people would have to look for, just some small pieces of paper or whatever. It could be like a treasure hunt. And if people collect a certain number of the pieces of paper, they win something like a discount or an inexpensive bookmark. Putting one idea inside the other. I continued really getting a feel for this box. That supervised area for kids might not work out as an everyday thing. I'd have to devote at least one or two people to it full time. But what if I nested the idea of an invitation only day or evening with supervised childcare on that day as a special reward to my best customers? That could work. That's the idea, responded Tom. Keep moving around. Go from macro to micro. Big picture to little. Specific to general. Tangible to abstract. Go back to an earlier idea. Let one thing make you think of something else. In fact, would you like me to let you in on one of the biggest secrets of problem solving and of coming up with ideas? Sure, I answered with trepidation. Though Tom had presented the idea boxes in easy to understand ways until now, I couldn't help but worry that his techniques might soon go beyond my reach. I can tell you in two words, Tom announced dramatically. The secret is more faster. More what faster ideas. I'm talking about coming up with a large huge volume of ideas quickly before your inner critic has time to pass judgment. In fact, let's try something right now. Give me a problem you need to solve. Anything. Even something relatively mundane. Well, our storeroom is always a mess, I admitted. I've tried and tried, but I just can't get the team to keep it neat or straighten up after themselves. Good, can't get much more mundane than that. Tom glanced at his watch before handing me a pen and a stack of paper. Okay, next challenge. You've got exactly 10 minutes to come up with 100 ideas on how to solve your storeroom problem. I can't come up with that many ideas. Tom raised his eyebrows again, challenging me again. Yes, you can, because I don't care if they're good ideas or bad ideas. In fact, I want you to come up with some bad ideas. Not only do you have permission, it's a requirement. I don't care if it's an old idea or a new idea. And I don't care whether it's an idea you think will work or one you think won't. I sat back, resigning myself to my fate. Tom continued, what we're shooting for here is volume, pure and simple. This is really thinking inside the box, a big box labeled 100 ideas that can be good, bad or indifferent. And remember to keep your sense of humor. Use the boxes and let one idea lead to another. Got it? Got it. I replied, feeling strangely free of any performance anxiety and anxious to see what my 100 ideas would be. Ready, set, go. And off I went. Here's what my list looked like when I was done. At which point, Tom handed me a yellow highlighter with the instructions to mark the ones worth exploring further. One, give prizes to whomever keeps stockroom neatest. Two, punish whoever doesn't. Whippings? Three, robot to clean room so don't need people. Four, self stacking books. Five, self-destructing trash. Six, self-emptying trash cans. Seven, no storeroom. Eight, change my attitude about messiness. Nine, all books online. 10, paperless office. 11, eliminate need for shelving books. 12, trash all books as they arrive. 13, hire only anal retentive employees, neat freaks. 14, full-time person in charge of cleaning, shelving. 15, part-time person in charge. 16, intern in charge. 17, automatic stacking and shelving machine. 18, divided more, more shelves, more subdivisions of where things go. 19, more trash cans. 20, bribes. 21, and highlighted. Nominal cash reward or small prizes to everyone when I see neat storeroom. 22, give out candy when neat. 23, salary deductions when messy. 24, and highlighted. New dimensions, sensory. Install good sound system with decent music so people will want to spend time in storeroom. Turn off when not neat. Carrot and stick. 25, turn up heat in storeroom. 26, make storeroom more visually appealing. Paint. 27, nice smells in storeroom. 28, gamify keeping storeroom neat. Give points. 29, prizes to whoever keeps neatest. 30, prizes to team who keeps storeroom neatest. 31, and highlighted. Every day, a fun 20-minute storeroom cleanup blitz. 32, take pictures of messy storeroom and display. Humiliation factor. 33, and highlighted. Take pictures of neat storeroom and display. Pride factor. 34, make storeroom bigger. 35, and highlighted. Make it smaller, less area to clean mess up. 36, turn storeroom upside down. Put shelves on ceiling. 37, do the opposite. Deliberately make messy? Don't store stuff in storeroom. 38, divide storeroom into sections. Make a place for everything. 39, prohibit anyone from going into storeroom. 40, have only one person allowed to go in, so responsibility not dispersed. 41, fixed to movable. Shelving on rollers? Boxes easier to move? Moveable storeroom? 42, changing to unchanging. Things don't get moved around. Everything stays where it is or gets thrown out or shelved. 43, remove what causes trouble. Don't allow books in storeroom. 44, and highlighted. Less extreme. Don't allow anything in storeroom that shouldn't be there. Books that should be shelved or sent back to publishers, papers that should be filed, miscellaneous items that shouldn't be there at all, etc. 45, extract what works. Put Jenny in charge of storeroom, since she does value neatness. 46, bring things together. Combine lunchroom and storeroom. Have to live with mess. 47, combine my office with storeroom, so I can keep an eye on it. As you can see, I didn't quite make it to 100, but it's a few dozen more ideas than I would have thought myself capable of coming up with in such a short time Let's see what we've got, said Tom when my time was up. I'm glad to see you're taking advantage of some of the boxes we've talked about. Hmm, that one's a bit weak, but not terrible. That one's okay. That's very funny. This one might have some potential, and we could combine this one and this one. And before I knew it, we had three or four darn good ideas that I couldn't wait to implement. Ideas that never would have occurred to me otherwise. And all in just 10 minutes. I want to open more boxes. I said feeling like a kid at Christmas. I can't wait to see what's in the next one. Tom seemed pleased with my enthusiasm. Here's a little something to take home with you. Would you like to try to solve a mystery? Sure. A dairy that delivers its milk by tanker truck had a problem. Their customers, big milk processors, were consistently complaining that they were a couple of gallons short. The company eliminated all possibility of error, leakage or evaporation. They had one driver and there was no doubt that their driver was stealing the milk. So they had private detectives follow the truck. The detectives watched him arrive at the dairy farm and fill up the big tanker truck. They tailed him as he drove to the processing plants. They watched as the processing plants crews pumped out their milk from the tanker. The driver never stopped en route. There were no visual clues as to how the driver had siphoned off or taken a drop of milk from the tank. Yet customers kept complaining they were short a few gallons. Now, here's the mystery for you to solve. How was that trucker stealing the milk?
